text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY — FOLLOW-UP OFFICE VISIT
Restaging Assessment: mCRPC on Enzalutamide


CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) presenting for restaging assessment following approximately 5 months of treatment with enzalutamide. Today's visit focuses on response evaluation, toxicity review, and review of recently completed restaging bone scan and CT imaging.


HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski is well known to our GU oncology service. To briefly summarize his history: he was initially diagnosed approximately 3 years ago at age 68 with de novo metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC), Gleason 4+5 = 9 (Grade Group 5), with bone metastases to L3, L4, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck, plus a single right obturator lymph node metastasis. His PSA at diagnosis was 187 ng/mL. He underwent germline testing which identified a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift mutation (c.5946delT), and somatic NGS revealed biallelic BRCA2 loss, TP53 p.R248W, PTEN deletion, RB1 heterozygous deletion, and MYC gain, among other alterations. He was initiated on ADT with leuprolide plus abiraterone/prednisone (LATITUDE/STAMPEDE intensification), achieving a PSA nadir of 0.8 ng/mL with radiographic improvement on restaging imaging at 18 months.

At approximately 24 months on ADT/abiraterone, he developed biochemical progression fulfilling PCWG3 criteria — PSA rose from a nadir of 0.8 to 4.7 ng/mL on three consecutive measurements — with castrate testosterone maintained below 20 ng/dL. PSMA PET/CT at that time demonstrated progression with new osseous metastases (T10 vertebral body, right 7th rib, left 9th rib, right sacral ala) as well as increased avidity at known L3/L4 lesions and an enlarging right obturator node (1.4 cm). Given CRPC with bone-dominant disease, prior AR pathway intensification with abiraterone, intact performance status (ECOG 1), and no visceral crisis, he was transitioned to enzalutamide 160 mg daily approximately 5 months ago. Leuprolide was continued for ongoing castration. Denosumab 120 mg subcutaneously every 4 weeks was added at that time for bone protection. Calcium 500 mg twice daily and vitamin D 1000 IU daily are ongoing.

At his 2-month follow-up, PSA had declined to 1.9 ng/mL (a 59% reduction from the 4.7 ng/mL baseline), consistent with an early biochemical response. He reported Grade 2 fatigue and mild cognitive slowing (Grade 1) at that visit; a single fall episode (no injury) was also reported, prompting physical therapy referral and fall prevention counseling. No dose reductions were required at that time.

Since that visit, the patient reports that his energy levels have improved somewhat. He denies new falls or near-fall events. He continues to work with his physical therapist and has found this helpful. His back pain, which had been a source of significant distress early in his CRPC course, has improved substantially and is now well-managed with scheduled acetaminophen 650 mg three times daily as needed. He has not required opioids. He denies worsening of any cognitive symptoms. Hot flashes remain a nuisance but are manageable with low-dose megestrol acetate (per prior recommendation).

He denies chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or changes in bowel or bladder habits. No hematuria. No new bone pain in areas beyond his known disease sites. No neurological symptoms, no weakness or numbness in the lower extremities, no bowel or bladder incontinence that would raise concern for cord compression.


REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

- Constitutional: Fatigue improved from Grade 2 to Grade 1. Weight stable. No fever, chills, or night sweats.
- HEENT: No new headaches, no visual changes.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, no palpitations, no peripheral edema. Blood pressure has been running in the 130s/80s at home per patient report.
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea at rest or with mild exertion. No cough.
- Gastrointestinal: Appetite intact. No nausea or vomiting. No abdominal pain. Bowel habits regular.
- Genitourinary: Urinary frequency unchanged (baseline BPH symptoms, stable). No hematuria.
- Musculoskeletal: Back pain significantly improved compared to onset of CRPC. Grade 1 discomfort at L3-L4 region with activity; well-controlled with scheduled acetaminophen. No new joint or bone pain. No hip pain.
- Neurologic: Mild word-finding difficulty reported (Grade 1, consistent with prior enzalutamide-associated cognitive effect). No focal neurological deficits. No falls since last visit.
- Endocrine: Hot flashes present, Grade 1, managed with megestrol.
- Psychiatric: Mood stable. Patient engaged and motivated. Denies depression or anxiety.
- Dermatologic: No rash.


PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 132/78 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 36.8°C, SpO2 97% on room air. Weight 81.2 kg (stable from last visit). BMI 26.4 kg/m².

General: Mr. Kowalski is a well-appearing, well-nourished elderly male in no acute distress. He is alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Conversational, cooperative, and pleasant. Moves about the room without assistance.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal, round, and reactive. Conjunctivae mildly pale. Sclerae anicteric.

Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpable.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema. Peripheral pulses intact bilaterally.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, rales, or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly palpated. No mass. Normoactive bowel sounds throughout.

Musculoskeletal: Lumbar spine non-tender to palpation at L3/L4 region. Range of motion of the lumbar spine limited by mild discomfort with flexion but improved from prior visits. No right hip tenderness to palpation. No obvious deformity. Gait steady without assistance.

Extremities: No clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. No focal bony tenderness of the iliac crests, ribs, or sacrum on palpation.

Neurologic: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in all four extremities. Sensation intact to light touch in bilateral lower extremities. Reflexes 2+ bilaterally. No clonus. Gait assessment: steady, cautious gait; no ataxia. Romberg negative.

Lymph nodes: No axillary, inguinal, or supraclavicular adenopathy appreciated on exam.


LABORATORY RESULTS

PSA: 0.6 ng/mL — this represents the nadir on enzalutamide (down from 4.7 ng/mL at baseline, representing an 87% reduction from baseline; down from 1.9 ng/mL at the 2-month check).

Testosterone: <20 ng/dL — confirms maintained castrate state on ongoing leuprolide.

CBC:
  - Hemoglobin: 11.4 g/dL (mild anemia, stable; likely multifactorial — ADT-related, anemia of chronic disease)
  - WBC: 6.4 K/uL (within normal limits)
  - Platelets: 210 K/uL (adequate)
  - Neutrophil count: 3.9 K/uL (adequate)

Comprehensive metabolic panel:
  - Sodium 139 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, CO2 25 mEq/L, Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL (baseline renal function stable)
  - BUN 18 mg/dL
  - AST 28 U/L, ALT 24 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 94 U/L (stable; mild ALP elevation consistent with known bone metastases)
  - Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL
  - Albumin 3.9 g/dL
  - Glucose 108 mg/dL (fasting; consistent with well-controlled T2DM)

HbA1c (last check 8 weeks ago): 6.9% — well-controlled type 2 diabetes.

Lipid panel (last drawn 4 months ago): LDL 88 mg/dL on statin — at goal.

Vitamin D 25-OH: 38 ng/mL — adequate on supplementation.

LH/FSH: Suppressed — consistent with leuprolide-mediated castration.


IMAGING RESULTS

Bone scan (technetium-99m, obtained 1 week ago): Previously identified areas of increased radiotracer uptake at L3, L4, T10, right femoral neck, right iliac wing, right sacral ala, right 7th rib, and left 9th rib are all present and unchanged in distribution. The intensity of several lesions, particularly the thoracic and rib sites, appears reduced compared to the PSMA PET/CT obtained approximately 5 months ago. No new foci of abnormal radiotracer uptake are identified. The overall pattern is consistent with stable osseous disease, with sclerotic evolution at several sites suggestive of ongoing treatment response.

CT abdomen/pelvis without and with IV contrast (obtained 1 week ago, read in conjunction with bone scan): The right obturator lymph node measures 0.9 cm in short axis, down from 1.4 cm at the time of the PSMA PET/CT that confirmed CRPC progression 5 months ago. This continues a trend of nodal response. No new pelvic or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy identified. The liver, spleen, kidneys, adrenal glands, and pancreas appear unremarkable. No hepatic lesions. No peritoneal implants. No free fluid. The bladder appears normal (patient is on ADT with an atrophied prostate). No new masses.

Overall impression of imaging: Stable-to-partial response at 5 months on enzalutamide. No evidence of new osseous lesions, no visceral metastases, and continued reduction in the right obturator node.


ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. METASTATIC CASTRATION-RESISTANT PROSTATE CANCER (mCRPC) — RESPONDING TO ENZALUTAMIDE

Mr. Kowalski is doing well on enzalutamide 5 months into treatment. He has achieved a PSA nadir of 0.6 ng/mL, representing an 87% reduction from his baseline at enzalutamide initiation (4.7 ng/mL). Restaging imaging confirms stable-to-partial response: no new bone lesions, stability of all previously identified osseous sites, and a reduction in the right obturator node (now 0.9 cm, down from a peak of 1.4 cm). Notably, no visceral disease has emerged, which remains reassuring. Clinically, the patient's back pain has improved significantly and his performance status remains ECOG 1, which is gratifying given the extent of his osseous disease burden at the time of CRPC diagnosis.

Plan:
- Continue enzalutamide 160 mg orally once daily. No dose reduction indicated at this time; toxicities are manageable.
- Continue leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months (next injection due in 6 weeks — patient to schedule with nursing).
- Continue denosumab 120 mg SQ every 4 weeks. Confirm dental clearance is up to date (patient was cleared at initiation; next dental check recommended in 6 months). Continue calcium 500 mg BID and vitamin D 1000 IU daily to reduce risk of osteonecrosis of the jaw and hypocalcemia.
- Next PSA, testosterone, and CBC in 4 weeks.
- Next restaging imaging (bone scan + CT A/P vs. PSMA PET/CT) in approximately 4 months, or sooner if clinical or biochemical concern for progression arises.
- I have informed the patient that enzalutamide responses at his disease stage and with his molecular profile (including the BRCA2 mutation) can be variable in duration. We discussed in general terms that if progression were to occur on enzalutamide, olaparib (Lynparza) — a PARP inhibitor approved for BRCA-mutated mCRPC — would be a strong consideration for subsequent therapy, given his confirmed germline and somatic biallelic BRCA2 loss and the PROfound trial data supporting PARP inhibitor use in this setting. Patient acknowledged and expressed understanding.

2. ENZALUTAMIDE TOXICITY MANAGEMENT

Fatigue has improved from Grade 2 to Grade 1. The patient's cognitive symptoms (mild word-finding difficulty) remain stable at Grade 1 and have not progressed. He has had no falls since his last visit and has been actively engaged with his physical therapist.
- Encourage continued physical therapy for fall prevention, muscle strengthening, and fatigue management.
- Reinforce fall precautions at home, including removal of loose rugs and use of grab bars.
- Continue megestrol acetate for hot flash management.
- No dose modification required at this time.

3. ANEMIA (MILD, GRADE 1)

Hemoglobin is 11.4 g/dL, mildly low, likely reflecting a combination of ADT-related erythropoiesis suppression and anemia of chronic disease in the setting of metastatic prostate cancer. This is stable and the patient is asymptomatic.
- Monitor CBC at next follow-up.
- Iron studies to be checked today (pending at time of this note) to ensure no concurrent iron deficiency.
- No transfusion indicated at this time. Erythropoiesis-stimulating agent not appropriate in the active malignancy context.

4. HYPERTENSION

Blood pressure at today's visit is 132/78 mmHg — well-controlled. The patient was already on an adjusted antihypertensive regimen (per prior note at the 12-month ADT/abiraterone visit). Continue current BP regimen (amlodipine 5 mg daily per medication list); no adjustments needed today.

5. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS

HbA1c 6.9% at last check, consistent with good glycemic control. Managed by his primary care physician. Continue metformin per PCP management. We discussed the impact of ADT on insulin sensitivity; patient is aware and is monitoring his blood glucose at home.

6. HYPERLIPIDEMIA

LDL 88 mg/dL on statin — at goal for this patient population. Continue current statin therapy.

7. BONE HEALTH / OSTEOPOROSIS PREVENTION

Patient is on denosumab, calcium, and vitamin D as above. ADT is a significant contributor to bone loss; denosumab provides both anti-tumor and bone-protective benefit in this setting.

8. GOALS OF CARE / PSYCHOSOCIAL

Briefly discussed the overall disease trajectory with Mr. Kowalski and his wife, who accompanied him today. He remains in a treatment-response phase and is stable. He understands this is a chronic, ultimately incurable illness but expressed gratitude for the current treatment response. He remains optimistic and engaged. No formal palliative care referral needed at this time given stable functional status and adequate symptom control; however, will reassess at each visit.

FOLLOW-UP: Return in 4 weeks for PSA/CBC check and toxicity assessment. Patient instructed to contact clinic immediately for new bone pain, urinary symptoms, lower extremity weakness/numbness, shortness of breath, or any other concerning symptoms.

Time spent: approximately 35 minutes, majority of time spent in counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed by:
Attending Physician, Genitourinary Medical Oncology
